reference: GATE CS — Computer Organization and Architecture / Instruction Set Architecture

map.condition: Effective address computed from PC plus displacement
Expanding opcode — count zero-address instructions available
Array element access via base + index

map.use: \text{Operands implicitly on TOS; no address field needed}

formulas.name: Immediate Addressing
formulas.latex: EA = \text{(operand is the constant in instruction itself)}
formulas.plain: Operand = constant embedded in instruction; no memory access needed
formulas.whenToUse: Instruction carries a literal constant as the operand (e.g. ADD R1, #5)
formulas.trap: No memory access for operand fetch — but the instruction itself must be fetched from memory.

formulas.name: Direct (Absolute) Addressing
formulas.plain: EA = address field A in the instruction
formulas.whenToUse: Operand is at the memory address directly specified in the instruction
formulas.trap: Address is absolute — code is NOT position-independent; relocation is required.

formulas.name: Indirect Addressing
formulas.plain: EA = contents of memory location A
formulas.whenToUse: The address field points to a memory location that holds the actual effective address

terms.means: value stored at memory address A

formulas.trap: Requires two memory accesses (one to get EA, one to get operand) — slower than direct.

formulas.name: Register (Direct) Addressing
formulas.plain: EA = register Ri specified in instruction
formulas.whenToUse: Operand is in a CPU register; fastest access, no memory reference for operand
formulas.trap: Register indirect is different — register holds an address, not the operand itself.

formulas.name: Register Indirect Addressing
formulas.plain: EA = memory location whose address is stored in register Ri
formulas.whenToUse: Register holds a pointer; useful for dynamic data structures and arrays

terms.means: memory contents at address held in register Ri

formulas.trap: One memory access still needed for operand; confused with register direct.

formulas.name: Displacement (Base) Addressing
formulas.plain: EA = offset A + base register R
formulas.whenToUse: Accessing fields of a struct/record where R is the base address and A is the field offset
formulas.trap: Base (displacement) addressing enables position-independent data access, but NOT code unless the base is PC.

formulas.name: Indexed Addressing
formulas.plain: EA = base address A + index register I
formulas.whenToUse: Traversing arrays: A is the array base and I is incremented to walk through elements

terms.means: index register value (auto-incremented for array walk)

formulas.trap: In some ISAs the index register and base register are swapped in notation — read the definition carefully.

formulas.name: PC-Relative (Relative) Addressing — Position Independence
formulas.plain: EA = PC + signed offset in instruction
formulas.whenToUse: Branches, jumps, and position-independent code; offset is relative to current PC
formulas.trap: GATE frequently asks which mode enables position-independent code — answer is PC-relative (relative) mode.

formulas.name: Stack (Implied / Zero-Address) Addressing
formulas.plain: Operand is implicitly the top of stack; SP is auto-adjusted on push/pop
formulas.whenToUse: Zero-address (stack) machines; expression evaluation using postfix notation
formulas.trap: Stack addressing is called 'implied' because no explicit operand address appears — do not confuse with immediate.

formulas.name: Addressing Mode — Classic Matches
formulas.plain: Base -> position-independent; Indexed -> arrays; Stack -> zero-address; Implied -> accumulator
formulas.whenToUse: Match-the-pair or MCQ questions mapping addressing mode to its primary use-case
formulas.trap: GATE 2014 match-the-pair: 'base addressing → position-independent' and 'stack → reentrant' are common confusions.

formulas.name: Basic Instruction Format Fields
formulas.plain: Instruction = opcode (k bits) | address1 (a bits) | address2 (a bits) | ...
formulas.whenToUse: Determine how many bits remain for opcode vs operands given total instruction length

terms.means: number of bits per address field

formulas.trap: Total bits = opcode + all address fields; increasing address field size reduces opcode space.

formulas.name: Expanding Opcode — Maximum Instructions per Level
formulas.latex: \text{Max n-address instructions at level } = 2^{k} - \text{(codes reserved for longer opcodes)}
formulas.plain: Available opcodes at each level = total codes at that opcode length minus those reserved for next level
formulas.whenToUse: Determine how many instructions of each type fit when expanding opcode technique is used

terms.means: opcode bits at the current level

formulas.trap: Each opcode reserved for the next level 'costs' the entire sub-tree below it — one 3-bit prefix removes 2^(remaining) zero-address slots.

formulas.name: Zero-Address Instructions Available (Expanding Opcode Formula)
formulas.plain: N0 = 2^n - (num_2addr)*2^(n-p) - (num_1addr)*2^(n-2p) - (num_0addr_prefixes)
formulas.whenToUse: Count remaining zero-address slots after reserving prefixes for 2-address and 1-address instructions
formulas.trap: Subtract reserved prefixes first; forgetting to account for prefix cascades is the most common GATE error here.

formulas.name: RISC Fixed-Length Instruction Format
formulas.plain: RISC uses fixed-length instructions; expanding opcode is a CISC (NOT RISC) technique
formulas.whenToUse: True/False: 'Expanding opcode is used in RISC' — answer is False

terms.means: fixed instruction length (typically 32 bits in RISC)

formulas.trap: Expanding opcode is a CISC feature enabling variable instruction length. RISC avoids it for simpler decode.

formulas.name: Instruction Address Types (0/1/2/3 address machines)
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{3-address:} & \text{OP } D, A, B \\ \text{2-address:} & \text{OP } D, A \\ \text{1-address:} & \text{OP } A \;(\text{ACC implicit}) \\ \text{0-address:} & \text{OP (stack)} \end{array}
formulas.plain: 3-addr: OP D A B | 2-addr: OP D A | 1-addr: OP A (ACC) | 0-addr: OP (TOS)
formulas.whenToUse: Trace assembly programs; determine how many instructions to evaluate an expression
formulas.trap: A 1-address machine uses an implicit accumulator — LOAD/STORE are still required to move values to/from memory.

formulas.name: 1-Address (Accumulator) Program Evaluation
formulas.latex: \text{LOAD } B \Rightarrow ACC \leftarrow M[B]; \quad \text{MULT } C \Rightarrow ACC \leftarrow ACC \times M[C]
formulas.plain: LOAD B: ACC = M[B]; MULT C: ACC = ACC * M[C]; STORE T: M[T] = ACC
formulas.whenToUse: Trace single-address assembly to derive the arithmetic expression computed
formulas.trap: Operations like ADD and MULT implicitly use ACC as one operand AND the destination — easy to miscode in traces.

formulas.name: XOR Register Swap (3-step)
formulas.latex: R_1 \leftarrow R_1 \oplus R_2; \quad R_2 \leftarrow R_1 \oplus R_2; \quad R_1 \leftarrow R_1 \oplus R_2
formulas.plain: R1 ^= R2; R2 ^= R1; R1 ^= R2  (swaps R1 and R2 without a temporary register)
formulas.whenToUse: Swap two registers using only XOR instructions with no temporary storage
formulas.trap: If R1 and R2 are the same register, all three XOR steps produce 0 — swapping a register with itself zeroes it.

formulas.name: Basic Instruction Execution Cycle
formulas.plain: Fetch -> Decode -> Operand Fetch -> Execute -> Write-back
formulas.whenToUse: Reason about number of memory accesses, pipeline stages, and clock cycles per instruction

terms.means: read instruction from memory using PC

formulas.trap: Indirect addressing adds an extra memory access in the Operand Fetch stage — doubles memory traffic vs direct.

formulas.name: Memory Accesses per Addressing Mode
formulas.plain: Immediate: 0 mem refs | Direct/Reg-indirect: 1 | Indirect: 2
formulas.whenToUse: Compare performance of addressing modes; count total memory accesses in an instruction
formulas.trap: Instruction fetch itself is always 1 memory access — do not double-count it with the operand access.

formulas.name: RISC Characteristics
formulas.latex: \text{RISC: } \begin{cases} \text{Few instructions, fixed length} \\ \text{Register-to-register ALU ops only} \\ \text{Load/Store architecture} \\ \text{Many registers} \\ \text{Hardwired control unit} \\ \text{Simple/few addressing modes} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: RISC: fixed-length instructions, reg-reg ALU, load/store only, many registers, hardwired CU, few addressing modes
formulas.whenToUse: MCQ asking which features characterize RISC; all of I/II/III in sample Q2
formulas.trap: RISC does NOT necessarily have fewer total instructions than CISC programs — it may need MORE instructions to do the same task.

formulas.name: CISC Characteristics
formulas.latex: \text{CISC: } \begin{cases} \text{Many complex instructions} \\ \text{Variable-length instruction format} \\ \text{Many addressing modes} \\ \text{Microprogrammed control} \\ \text{Memory-to-memory ops possible} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: CISC: complex/variable instructions, many addressing modes, microprogrammed CU, mem-to-mem ops
formulas.whenToUse: Contrast with RISC; questions about which processor type uses expanding opcodes or microprogramming
formulas.trap: CISC's expanding opcode is designed to support many different instruction types with variable-length encoding — not used in RISC.

formulas.name: What ISA Includes
formulas.latex: \text{ISA} = \{\text{Registers visible to programmer, } \text{Instruction set, Addressing modes, Data types, } \text{I/O model}\}
formulas.plain: ISA includes: programmer-visible registers, instructions, addressing modes, data types, I/O model
formulas.whenToUse: Identify which items belong to ISA vs microarchitecture (cache size, clock speed are NOT ISA)
formulas.trap: Cache size, clock frequency, and number of pipeline stages are microarchitecture details — NOT part of ISA.

formulas.name: What ISA Does NOT Include
formulas.latex: \text{NOT ISA: } \{\text{Cache size, Clock frequency, Pipeline depth, Number of ALUs}\}
formulas.plain: Cache size, clock speed, pipeline depth, number of ALUs are implementation/microarchitecture, not ISA
formulas.whenToUse: Eliminate wrong options in ISA-scope MCQs
formulas.trap: Number of registers is ISA (programmer-visible); size of cache is not — a common GATE distractor.

formulas.name: Activation Record (Stack Frame) Layout
formulas.plain: Stack frame contains: return address | saved registers | local variables | parameters
formulas.whenToUse: Reason about function call overhead, stack pointer movement, and frame pointer usage

terms.means: frame pointer (base of current activation record)

formulas.trap: Each nested function call pushes a new frame; forgetting to pop on return corrupts the stack.

formulas.name: Reentrant Code Property
formulas.latex: \text{Reentrant code: no self-modification; shared safely by multiple processes}
formulas.plain: Reentrant code does not modify itself and can be shared by concurrent processes without conflict
formulas.whenToUse: Identify which addressing mode / code property allows safe sharing (stack addressing enables reentrant routines)
formulas.trap: Position-independent code (relative addressing) and reentrant code are related but distinct concepts — position-independence is about relocatability, reentrancy is about safe concurrent sharing.
